markdown.markdown:
This is not simply a record of positions held or papers published.

It is the story of how one landscape led to another, how one question became many, and how a career gradually evolved from studying ecosystems to exploring the ecology of the mind.

---


## University of Florida

**Ph.D. in Interdisciplinary Ecology (2015)**

Concentration in Geographic Information Systems.

Dissertation:

*Metrics of Pattern Loss and Ecosystem Change in the Ridge and Slough Mosaic of the Everglades*

Advisor:

**Matthew J. Cohen**

Here I learned that landscapes are not collections of objects, but systems of relationships.

---

## Beijing Forestry University

**M.Sc. in Natural Resources Conservation (2009)**

This was where ecology became more than biology.
It became a way of understanding human society and environmental change.

---

## Southwest Forestry University

**B.Sc. in Environmental Science (2005)**

My first introduction to environmental science and field investigation.

---

# Where I Worked

## California Department of Water Resources

**Senior Environmental Scientist**
2022–Present

Working at the intersection of land use, water resources, cloud computing, reproducible science, and AI-assisted environmental workflows.

Current interests include

- Environmental Data Infrastructure
- Reproducible Science
- Cloud-native Geospatial Computing
- AI-assisted Scientific Publishing

---

## Michigan State University

**Research Associate**
2020–2022

Studied land use change, ecosystem functions, and sustainability across the Asian Drylands using cloud computing and Google Earth Engine.

---

## University of Maine

**Research Associate**
2018–2020

Worked on social-ecological systems, marine spatial planning, and geospatial data integration.

---

## Chinese Academy of Environmental Planning

**Researcher**
2015–2017

Focused on natural resource damage assessment (NRDA), environmental accounting, and Gross Ecosystem Product (GEP).

---

# What I Study

My work sits at the intersection of several disciplines.

- Landscape Ecology
- Remote Sensing
- Environmental Data Science
- GIScience
- Systems Thinking
- Science Communication
- Artificial Intelligence for Environmental Research

Rather than treating these as separate fields, I see them as different scales of understanding the same world.

---

# Ideas in Print

Selected publications that represent turning points in my thinking.

### Remote Sensing of Environment (2020)

Remote detection of ecosystem degradation in the Everglades ridge–slough landscape.

---

### Landscape Ecology (2015)

Linking landscape pattern to hydrological processes.

---

### Applied Geography (2021)

Spatial patterns of socio-demographic change in coastal Maine.

---

### Remote Sensing (2022)

Land use hotspots across Kazakhstan and Mongolia.

---

### Scientific Reports (2024)

Disproportionate contributions of land cover and changes to ecosystem functions.

---

*A complete publication list is available on Google Scholar.*

[→ Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=Jkgly20AAAAJ&hl=en){.essay-card-link}

---

# Beyond Research

Science is also about building communities.

Along the way I have been fortunate to contribute through

- mentoring students
- teaching GIS
- organizing scientific workshops
- reviewing manuscripts
- building open scientific tools

I believe that knowledge grows most meaningfully when it is shared.

---

# How I Work

Technology is part of my scientific language.

Current tools include

- R
- Google Earth Engine
- JavaScript
- PostgreSQL/PostGIS
- Quarto
- Git/GitHub
- Cloud Computing
- AI-assisted Research

I am increasingly interested in how modern software practices can reshape environmental science.

---

# Looking Forward

Thinking Across Scales is perhaps my most ambitious project.

It brings together years of field ecology, computational science, writing, photography, and interdisciplinary thinking into a single evolving body of work.

Rather than treating research, art, and public communication as separate pursuits, I hope this project demonstrates that they are simply different ways of observing the same world.

---

## Download

[Download CV →](cv.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-dark target="_blank"}
